topic: AI Ethics, Bias, and Explainable AI (XAI)

questions.scenario: An automated credit underwriting platform built on an XGBoost classifier processes 500 loan applications per minute. The compliance department demands local, contrastive explanations for every rejected application within a strict 30ms SLA. The engineering team implements Path-Dependent TreeSHAP to calculate exact local feature attributions (Shapley values) rapidly. During auditing, they observe that highly correlated features (e.g., 'Annual Income' and 'Credit Limit') are receiving counter-intuitive attribution scores that do not align with domain-specific causal mechanisms, where a features irrelevant to the actual model decision is assigned a high importance score due to its correlation with a predictive feature.
questions.question: What is the primary technical trade-off and root cause of this anomaly?
options: A) Path-Dependent TreeSHAP calculates conditional expectations, which implicitly weights out-of-distribution (OOD) data points, resulting in misleading attributions for correlated features, despite meeting the 30ms SLA.
B) Path-Dependent TreeSHAP assumes strict feature independence, which fails to capture non-linear interactions between income and credit limit, thus violating the efficiency axiom of Shapley values.

C) The path-dependent algorithm relies on conditional expectations E[f(X)|X_S = x_S] which can attribute importance to a feature that the model does not actually use if it is highly correlated with an active feature, violating the 'dummy player' (or null player) axiom under a causal interpretation.
D) The 30ms SLA forces the algorithm to truncate the tree depth during attribution mapping, causing a loss of precision in the calculated Shapley values for late-splitting features.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) The path-dependent algorithm relies on conditional expectations E[f(X)|X_S = x_S] which can attribute importance to a feature that the model does not actually use if it is highly correlated with an active feature, violating the 'dummy player' (or null player) axiom under a causal interpretation.
questions.explanation: Path-Dependent TreeSHAP uses conditional expectations to estimate the effect of missing features. While this keeps calculations restricted to the true data manifold (avoiding out-of-distribution evaluations) and is computationally highly efficient ($O(TLD^2)$), it violates the 'dummy player' axiom. If Feature A (unused by the model) is highly correlated with Feature B (highly used), the conditional expectation will still shift when conditioning on Feature A, resulting in a non-zero Shapley value for Feature A. To resolve this, one must use Interventional TreeSHAP, which uses marginal expectations (requiring a background dataset), but this increases computation time to $O(N \cdot TLD^2)$, risking violation of the 30ms SLA.

questions.scenario: A health-tech company has trained a deep neural network to predict cardiovascular risk. The training dataset is heavily skewed, containing 85% data from Demographic Group A (majority) and 15% from Demographic Group B (minority). The model exhibits high overall accuracy but a significantly higher False Negative Rate (FNR) for Group B. The engineering team is tasked with achieving 'Equal Opportunity' (equal FNR/TPR across both groups). Because the model is deployed on edge devices in clinical environments, retraining is computationally prohibitive, and inference must occur in under 5ms.
questions.question: Which bias mitigation paradigm is optimal under these constraints, and what is its primary operational trade-off?
options: A) In-processing via Adversarial Debiasing, because it directly minimizes mutual information between the prediction and the sensitive attribute without increasing edge-device inference latency.

B) Pre-processing via Re-weighing, because modifying the dataset offline guarantees zero runtime performance overhead and maintains the mathematical integrity of the original neural network's parameters.

C) Post-processing via Equalized Odds threshold adjustment, because it requires no model retraining, runs in O(1) post-inference time by mapping group-specific decision thresholds, but trades off overall predictive utility (accuracy).

D) Post-processing via Reject Option Classification, because it uses a localized perturbation kernel around the decision boundary, maintaining exact demographic parity at the expense of doubling inference latency.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Post-processing via Equalized Odds threshold adjustment, because it requires no model retraining, runs in O(1) post-inference time by mapping group-specific decision thresholds, but trades off overall predictive utility (accuracy).
questions.explanation: Since retraining the model is computationally prohibitive, pre-processing and in-processing techniques are ruled out. Post-processing techniques (such as the Equalized Odds post-processor by Hardt et al.) work by adjusting the classification thresholds for different demographic groups post-inference. This requires zero model retraining and executes in $O(1)$ time (simple conditional threshold check), satisfying the strict 5ms edge latency constraint. The fundamental trade-off is utility: adjusting these thresholds away from the model's Bayes optimal decision boundary inevitably reduces the overall accuracy (utility) of the system.

questions.scenario: A retail credit risk model operates on a high-dimensional feature space containing over 500 features (comprising continuous, discrete, and sparse categorical representations). To comply with consumer protection regulations, the system must generate 'Counterfactual Explanations' (actionable recourses) for rejected applicants (e.g., 'If your debt-to-income ratio was 30% instead of 45%, you would have been approved'). However, searching a 500-dimensional mixed-type space for a valid, sparse, and actionable counterfactual instance is computationally expensive and frequently yields unrealistic changes (e.g., altering immutable features like age or country of birth).
questions.question: To implement a computationally viable and legally compliant counterfactual generation engine under these high-dimensional constraints, which algorithmic design pattern should the team select?
options: A) Perform a brute-force hyper-ellipsoid search using Mahalanobis distance across all 500 dimensions to guarantee finding the global minimum-distance counterfactual.

B) Apply Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce the 500-dimensional space to 10 latent dimensions, perform a gradient-descent search in this latent space, and reconstruct the counterfactual using the inverse PCA transform.

C) Formulate the search as a multi-objective optimization problem using a genetic algorithm or loss function that optimizes for L1-norm (sparsity), L2-norm (proximity), and utilizes a custom Gower distance-based feasibility constraint to lock immutable features.
D) Utilize an isotropic growing spheres generation method around the target instance, scaling the perturbation radius uniformly across all 500 dimensions until the decision boundary is crossed.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Formulate the search as a multi-objective optimization problem using a genetic algorithm or loss function that optimizes for L1-norm (sparsity), L2-norm (proximity), and utilizes a custom Gower distance-based feasibility constraint to lock immutable features.
questions.explanation: In high-dimensional spaces with mixed feature types, counterfactual generation must balance multiple competing objectives: proximity (how close the counterfactual is to the original point, measured via L2), sparsity (the number of features changed, measured via L1 so the user has actionable, simple steps), and feasibility (not changing immutable features like age, and respecting real-world correlations). Formulating this as a multi-objective loss function with Gower distance (to handle mixed categorical/continuous data) and hard constraints on immutable features is the industry standard (e.g., DiCE). PCA reconstruction (Option B) fails because inverse-transforming back to 500 dimensions changes almost all features simultaneously, violating the sparsity requirement. Brute-force (Option A) and growing spheres (Option D) suffer from the curse of dimensionality and cannot natively handle mixed-type data or immutability constraints.
